authoring_instructions: Use work_packet.instruction_text and work_packet.compiled_context for scientific judgment; this authoring contract supplies mechanics only.
Write one bare Transaction JSON object, not a candidate wrapper, at output_locations.candidate_logical_path; write helper files only below output_locations.shadow_logical_root.

Copy every field in transaction_bindings that also appears in transaction_json_schema exactly into the Transaction (binding_schema is contract metadata), including base_revision and created_at; choose only transaction_id, intent, preconditions, changed_facets, operations, evidence_refs, and authority_basis as the route and schemas require.
For each typed entity, put {schema: payload_schema_id, payload: <schema-valid object>} in owner_facet and set every listed empty_facet to an empty object.

Set every new EntityVersion and RelationVersion project_id, privacy, access_compartments, and created_at exactly to the corresponding transaction_bindings values; never rely on privacy or compartment defaults.
Use only output_contract allowed operation, entity, and relation types; satisfy every output cardinality and the exact scientific exit conditions in work_packet.instruction_text.
JSON Schema is necessary but not sufficient: obey output_contract.model_invariants exactly and use the bridge's structured candidate diagnostics for any remaining model-level repair.

Include exactly one route.outcome operation bound to transaction_bindings.route_run_id and transaction_bindings.route_id, with the same privacy and access compartments; candidate_refs must enumerate every exact canonical object produced by the Transaction, including any entity, relation, artifact, blocker, or other schema-permitted reference required by the route validator.
Do not fabricate a human decision or approval; obey every work_packet.forbidden_actions entry and stop if the route requires unavailable human authority.

The candidate source may be ordinary readable UTF-8 JSON and may end with a newline. The bridge validates it as a strict Transaction and computes the digest from engine-canonical Transaction bytes; do not hash the source file bytes or put a digest inside the object.

model_invariants.repair_hint: Use trace_only for provenance links; relation importance alone does not justify hard invalidation.
model_invariants.requirement: upstream and downstream must both be null

model_invariants.repair_hint: Either add both exact facet endpoints or change a provenance-only link to trace_only.
model_invariants.requirement: upstream must bind the exact source entity/version and downstream must bind the exact target entity/version

model_invariants.repair_hint: Choose exact scope equality or typed overlap evidence, but never both.
model_invariants.requirement: exactly one of scope_ref or scope_overlap is required; other modes must omit scope_overlap

model_invariants.repair_hint: Bind the exact immediately preceding immutable relation version.
model_invariants.requirement: version 1 omits supersedes; version n > 1 supersedes version n - 1 with the same relation_id

transaction_json_schema.description: Canonical transaction body.  Its digest is intentionally absent.
transaction_json_schema.title: Transaction

exact_primitives: Use the proposed-scope primitives without alteration.
A truthful certificate issued by a high-capacity seller remains public across periods until a buyer trades with that seller.
The buyer may inspect one uncertified seller at cost c, buy without inspection, or exit.
Certification and inspection strategies are equilibrium choices at every k.
benchmarks.label: Consumable certificates with endogenous inspection
benchmarks.prediction: This benchmark records the joint maps k to S(k), A(k), and U(k); their direction and shape are left unresolved.
benchmarks.solution_concept: Stationary Markov perfect equilibrium with short-lived buyer best response and one fixed equilibrium-selection rule used for every k and every benchmark.
timing: Each seller privately observes its current capacity.
Each high-capacity seller chooses whether to pay k for a truthful public certificate.
The arriving buyer observes the public certificate stock and chooses inspection, purchase without inspection, or exit.
Inspection, if chosen, reveals the current capacity of one uncertified seller before purchase.
Trade and service occur; a certificate is consumed exactly when its seller trades, while every unconsumed certificate carries to the next period.
Capacities transition and the next short-lived buyer arrives.
benchmarks.unresolved_delta: Whether the joint equilibrium contains a strict three-cost reversal and which counterfactual removes it.

exact_primitives: Use the baseline state transition, certificate issuance, carryover, depletion, allocation, and inspection realization laws generated at a declared reference cost k_0.
For counterfactual k, hold every seller certification rule, buyer inspection rule, purchase rule, and induced state-distribution law fixed at its k_0 value.
Replace only the certification payment k_0 in seller payoff accounting by k.
benchmarks.label: Mechanical certification-cost accounting
benchmarks.prediction: Buyer inspection, allocation, buyer surplus, and the certificate-stock law are fixed by construction; only the direct certification payment changes in seller payoff accounting.
benchmarks.solution_concept: Partial-equilibrium accounting counterfactual around the selected stationary baseline equilibrium at k_0; no strategy is reoptimized.
timing: Replay the baseline k_0 action and state path without behavioral reoptimization.
At each certificate issuance, charge the counterfactual k rather than k_0.
Leave buyer information, allocation, service, certificate consumption, and transitions unchanged.
benchmarks.unresolved_delta: The difference between the full equilibrium response and this fixed-path accounting response is behavioral, but is not yet assigned to stock or inspection feedback.

exact_primitives: Hold all baseline primitives, actions, information, and the equilibrium-selection rule fixed except certificate persistence.
A truthful certificate issued after current capacity is observed is public to the current buyer.
If it is not consumed by current trade, it expires before capacities transition and before the next buyer arrives.
Certification and buyer inspection remain endogenous at every k.
benchmarks.label: One-period truthful certificates
benchmarks.prediction: The baseline-minus-one-period difference isolates effects that require interperiod certificate carryover and trade-triggered depletion; no sign is posited.
benchmarks.solution_concept: Stationary Markov perfect equilibrium under the same equilibrium-selection rule as the baseline.
timing: Follow baseline timing through current-period trade.
Consume a certificate if its seller trades.
Expire every other current certificate at the end of the period before capacities transition.
Begin the next period with an empty inherited certificate stock.
benchmarks.unresolved_delta: Whether removing inherited certificate stock eliminates any strict three-cost reversal and the associated change in the uncertified-pool composition or inspection return.

exact_primitives: Use the baseline consumable-certificate state, issuance, carryover, and trade-triggered depletion laws.
Declare a reference cost k_0 and fix the buyer's inspection probability and inspected-seller selection rule after every public state at their selected baseline k_0 values for all counterfactual k.
Allow seller certification and buyer purchase-or-exit choices after the imposed inspection realization to respond to k.
Hold all remaining primitives and the equilibrium-selection rule fixed.
benchmarks.label: Consumable certificates with exogenous inspection rule
benchmarks.prediction: The baseline-minus-fixed-inspection difference isolates feedback through endogenous inspection; no direction for S(k), A(k), or U(k) is posited.
benchmarks.solution_concept: Stationary seller equilibrium and buyer purchase best response subject to the exogenously fixed public-state-contingent inspection rule, using the common equilibrium-selection rule.
timing: Sellers observe capacity and choose certification.
The buyer observes the certificate stock.
An inspection action is imposed using the public-state-contingent k_0 rule and, when imposed, reveals one uncertified seller's current capacity at cost c.
The buyer chooses purchase or exit, trade occurs, the traded seller's certificate is consumed, and unconsumed certificates carry over.
benchmarks.unresolved_delta: Whether an equilibrium reversal survives when the buyer's inspection margin cannot adjust with k.

payload.exact_question_delta: At the same primitive vector, k_L < k_M < k_H, and common equilibrium-selection rule: test the joint baseline for a strict middle-cost peak or trough in S, A, or U; compare it with the fixed-path accounting benchmark for the direct payment effect, with the one-period-certificate benchmark for interperiod stock and depletion, and with the fixed-inspection benchmark for endogenous inspection feedback.

entities.summary: Four matched environments isolate the joint equilibrium, the accounting effect of certification cost, interperiod certificate stock, and endogenous inspection feedback.
entities.title: Benchmarks for consumable certificate stock and endogenous inspection

edges.economic_meaning: Current high capacity is the eligibility condition for certification.
k enters the seller's certification tradeoff.
Issuance adds to the public certificate stock.
Intertemporal validity determines which inherited stock states are coherent.
Carryover and depletion determine the certificate configuration observed by the buyer.
Capacity persistence affects current capacity conditional on uncertified status.
Selective issuance and depletion sort sellers into the uncertified pool.
The uncertified posterior determines the information value of inspection.
Public alternatives affect the gain from learning an uncertified seller's capacity.
Which seller is inspected determines the information acquired.
The gross return is compared with c.
c is the private resource cost of inspection.
Inspection changes the information available for purchase or exit.
Trading with a certified seller consumes its certificate.
Buyer payoffs determine purchase and exit incentives.
Purchase occurs after public observation and any inspection.
The equilibrium induces the stationary outcome maps.
Common selection makes outcome comparisons well-defined.
The mechanical frozen path is copied from the selected k_0 equilibrium.
The frozen inspection probability and target rule are copied from k_0.
The mechanical benchmark freezes certification rather than reoptimizing it.
The one-period benchmark removes certificate carryover.
The fixed-inspection benchmark removes adjustment of the inspection rule to k.

nodes.economic_meaning: Two strategic sellers repeatedly face one buyer who chooses between them or exits and does not internalize future certificate-stock effects.
nodes.label: Two long-lived sellers and one short-lived buyer
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Exactly two sellers persist and one fresh buyer arrives per period.
This market size is structural scope and is unchanged by every benchmark.

nodes.economic_meaning: Each seller privately observes a binary current service-capacity state that determines certification eligibility and successful service.
nodes.label: Private current high or low capacity
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Only current high capacity permits truthful certification.
Uncertified status alone does not identify capacity.

nodes.economic_meaning: Serial and cross-seller dependence in capacity determines the stationary public state and the current composition of uncertified sellers.
nodes.label: Capacity transition kernel
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Specify one joint Markov kernel before formal analysis.
Hold that kernel fixed across k and all benchmarks.

nodes.economic_meaning: A high-capacity seller decides whether to pay k to add a truthful public certificate, trading off the payment against current and future demand.
nodes.label: Eligible seller certification choice
primitive_sufficient_conditions: The choice is endogenous in the consumable baseline, one-period-certificate, and fixed-inspection benchmarks.
The mechanical accounting benchmark freezes it at the selected reference equilibrium.

nodes.economic_meaning: The main comparative-static primitive is the private payment charged at certificate issuance.
nodes.label: Certification payment k
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Vary k while holding every other primitive fixed.
No sign of any response is imposed.

nodes.economic_meaning: An issued certificate remains visible until its seller trades, when it is consumed; a certificate not consumed carries into the next period.
nodes.label: Public carryover stock and trade-triggered depletion
primitive_sufficient_conditions: This rule applies in the consumable baseline, mechanical accounting, and fixed-inspection benchmarks.
The one-period benchmark replaces carryover by end-of-period expiry.

nodes.economic_meaning: A formal state must specify what an inherited certificate attests after a new capacity draw and whether a seller with an outstanding certificate may recertify.
nodes.label: Validity and recertification of inherited certificates
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Select one truth-preserving intertemporal semantics before formal implementation.
Use it identically in every benchmark with certificate carryover.

nodes.economic_meaning: The buyer observes which sellers currently carry public certificates before deciding whether to inspect, purchase, or exit.
nodes.label: Observed certificate-stock state
primitive_sufficient_conditions: The complete public state must distinguish every payoff-relevant certificate configuration.

nodes.economic_meaning: The buyer may pay c to reveal one uncertified seller's current capacity before selecting a purchase or exit.
nodes.label: Inspection of one uncertified seller at cost c
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Inspection is endogenous in the consumable and one-period benchmarks.
The fixed-inspection benchmark freezes its public-state-contingent probability and target rule.

nodes.economic_meaning: When more than one seller is uncertified, the model needs a tie-breaking or randomized rule identifying which seller is inspected, and the fixed-inspection benchmark must freeze that exact rule.
nodes.label: Selection of the inspected uncertified seller
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Declare a deterministic or stochastic target rule for every public state.
Use the same rule at the reference cost and in matched counterfactuals.

nodes.economic_meaning: The buyer compares c with the state-contingent gross value of learning an uncertified seller's capacity.
nodes.label: Inspection cost c
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Hold c fixed while k varies and across benchmarks.

nodes.economic_meaning: After public information and any inspection result, the buyer selects a seller or exits; a trade determines service and certificate depletion.
nodes.label: Purchase from a seller or exit
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Allow purchase without inspection, purchase after inspection, and exit.

nodes.economic_meaning: Successful service gives buyer value one, sellers earn a successful-trade margin, and sellers discount future profits; a complete model must state prices or transfers and failed-service payoffs.
nodes.label: Buyer and seller trade payoffs
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Treat buyer value one as the common payoff numeraire, not a benchmark channel.
Specify all remaining transfer and failure payoffs and hold them fixed across benchmarks.

nodes.economic_meaning: Within a period: sellers observe capacity; eligible sellers certify; the buyer observes certificates; inspection may occur; the buyer purchases or exits; service and payoffs occur; trade consumes a certificate; then capacities transition.
nodes.label: Capacity, certification, information, inspection, trade, depletion, transition
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Only the one-period benchmark inserts expiry of every unconsumed certificate after trade and before transition.

nodes.economic_meaning: Equilibrium issuance, persistence, and depletion sort sellers into the uncertified pool and determine its public-state-contingent capacity distribution.
nodes.label: Capacity distribution conditional on being uncertified
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Derive this distribution from strategies and the invariant state law.

nodes.economic_meaning: The value before paying c depends on the uncertified-pool posterior, the public alternatives, the target rule, and how information changes purchase or exit.
nodes.label: Gross value of inspection
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Compute it in each public state using the same payoff completion.

nodes.economic_meaning: Seller certification strategies, buyer inspection and purchase best responses, and an invariant distribution over the full public state jointly define a candidate stationary Markov equilibrium.
nodes.label: Stationary Markov equilibrium candidate
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Specify the full Markov state before asserting existence.
This node proposes a solution concept and does not validate it.

nodes.economic_meaning: One rule must select among multiple stationary equilibria for every k and benchmark so that comparisons do not reflect benchmark-specific selection.
nodes.label: Common equilibrium-selection rule
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Record the selection rule before evaluation and apply it without benchmark-specific discretion.

nodes.economic_meaning: S(k), A(k), and U(k) are derived under the selected stationary equilibrium and evaluated for a strict middle-cost peak or trough at common k_L < k_M < k_H.
nodes.label: Stationary inspection, allocation, and buyer-surplus maps
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Use the same primitive vector and selection rule for all three costs and benchmarks.
No comparative-static direction is presumed.

nodes.economic_meaning: The mechanical and fixed-inspection benchmarks require one exact admissible k_0 and its selected baseline equilibrium, from which frozen strategy and state-contingent rules are copied.
nodes.label: Declared reference cost k_0 and selected reference equilibrium
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Choose k_0 before constructing either frozen-rule benchmark.
Use the common equilibrium-selection rule at k_0.

nodes.economic_meaning: Freeze certification, inspection, purchase, certificate-stock, allocation, and state-distribution laws from k_0 and change only the certification payment in seller payoff accounting.
nodes.label: Fixed-path mechanical accounting
primitive_sufficient_conditions: No strategy or state law is reoptimized.

nodes.economic_meaning: Expire every unconsumed certificate before the next capacity transition while leaving certification and inspection endogenous.
nodes.label: One-period certificate expiry
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Change only interperiod certificate persistence.

nodes.economic_meaning: At every counterfactual k, impose the inspection probability and target rule selected at k_0 while allowing certification and post-inspection purchase or exit to respond.
nodes.label: Frozen public-state-contingent inspection
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Change only the endogeneity of inspection.

entities.summary: A refined proposal-only decomposition that explicitly identifies the common reference cost and inspection-target rule used by the exact benchmarks, alongside structural primitives, normalizations, derived objects, and unresolved formal scope choices.
entities.title: Primitive graph for consumable quality certificates

payload.importance: The answer determines whether lowering the private cost of producing truthful public information can have qualitatively different incidence once information is a durable but depletable stock and buyers can acquire substitute information. It identifies which behavioral margin must be modeled before certification-cost changes can be interpreted.
payload.kill_condition: Kill the broad question if, for every admissible primitive vector and the common equilibrium-selection rule, each of S(k), A(k), and U(k) is monotone on the admissible k domain. Conditional on finding non-monotonicity, kill the proposed consumable-stock explanation if the same strict k_L,k_M,k_H reversal is reproduced in the matched one-period-certificate benchmark and the baseline-minus-one-period difference in both the uncertified-pool capacity distribution and the gross return to inspection is zero at all three costs; classify a reversal reproduced with frozen certification and inspection rules as mechanical rather than a stock mechanism.
payload.object_to_explain: For a fully specified Markov completion of the two-seller environment and one common equilibrium-selection rule, let S(k) be the stationary probability that the arriving buyer pays c to inspect an uncertified seller, A(k) the stationary probability that any trade is with a currently high-capacity seller, and U(k) the arriving buyer's stationary expected value net of price and inspection cost. Is there an admissible primitive vector and k_L < k_M < k_H for which at least one X in {S,A,U} has X(k_M) strictly above both X(k_L) and X(k_H), or strictly below both, because certificate carryover and trade-triggered depletion change the capacity composition of the uncertified pool and hence the gross return to inspection?
payload.phenomenon: Possible non-monotonicity in the stationary buyer inspection probability, the probability that trade is allocated to a currently high-capacity seller, or expected buyer surplus net of inspection cost as certificate cost k falls.
prohibited_claims: No direction of any comparative static is claimed.
No existence, uniqueness, proof, validation, or welfare result is claimed.
No literature novelty claim is made.
No human acceptance or structural approval is inferred.
payload.proposed_scope: An infinite discrete-time two-seller model with one short-lived buyer per period; privately observed current high or low capacity; truthful certification available only to a high-capacity seller at cost k; a public certificate that persists until the certified seller trades and is then consumed; buyer actions of inspecting one uncertified seller at cost c, buying without inspection, or exiting; seller margins on successful trade; seller discounting; a fully specified capacity transition law; stationary Markov strategies; and one equilibrium-selection rule held fixed across all k values and benchmarks.
payload.unresolved_delta: Whether such a three-cost non-monotonicity exists in the consumable-certificate equilibrium and disappears in the matched benchmarks that separately remove interperiod certificate stock, freeze behavior to expose the accounting effect of k, and freeze buyer inspection to remove inspection feedback.

entities.summary: A proposal to test whether the stock and trade-triggered depletion of truthful certificates changes the uncertified pool enough to make key outcomes non-monotone in certification cost.
entities.title: Can cheaper consumable quality certificates generate non-monotone search or allocation?

content.authority: This route may explore and propose at L1. It cannot create human confirmation or alter a confirmed scientific scope.
content.budget_estimator: etai_lexical_v1 is deterministic lexical accounting, not a provider token count.
content.scope: Preserve exact assumptions, quantifiers, scope, dissent, and stale blockers. Generated context and candidates are noncanonical.
content.validity: Formal truth, economic interpretation, literature novelty, human acceptance, and freshness are distinct.

instructions: Decompose the framed environment into economically meaningful primitives, timing, information, actions, payoffs, and solution concept candidates.
Name which primitives differ from each exact benchmark and which are merely normalizations.
Produce a PrimitiveGraph and a G1 GateDossier with importance, kill conditions, scope risks, and exact supporting references.
Do not confirm G1; only a human decision may promote the question and benchmark scope.
Bind the G1 GateDossier to the exact ResearchQuestion ref, include that ref among its ordered objects, and keep every output in that one scope.
